title: External Flow
subtitle: MQL5-to-Python Bridge — HTTP POST of D1 OHLCV to Flask, Signal Returned as JSON
author: Christian Benjamin (lynnchris)
verdict: ARCHITECTURE REFERENCE — validates our broker-agnostic Python design. The bridge they build to reach Python is what we ARE from day one.

series_evolution.parts_1_8: Pure MQL5 tools — all logic and display inside MetaTrader
series_evolution.part_9: PIVOT: First hybrid tool. MQL5 EA collects data, Python does the analysis. The series author recognises MQL5 cannot do what Python can (Pandas, ML, complex analytics). This is the series acknowledging its own limitations and opening the door to the external world.
series_evolution.significance: This is the conceptual ancestor of every broker-adapter pattern. The fact that they need to build this bridge validates our decision to build entirely in Python with broker adapters from the start.

tool_classification.process_1: MQL5 EA — data collector and display

core_concept.name: External Processing Bridge
core_concept.description: MQL5 can collect data but cannot run Pandas, sklearn, or complex analytics. Python can, but cannot connect to a live broker. Bridge the two: MQL5 fetches market data, serialises it to CSV, POSTs it to a Python Flask endpoint, Python analyses and returns a signal JSON, MQL5 displays the result.

signal_logic.buy: last close > 10-day average of OHLC average price
signal_logic.sell: last close < 10-day average of OHLC average price
signal_logic.note: Extremely simple — the VALUE of this part is the bridge architecture, not the signal logic

core_concept.relevance_to_creviaderiv: We ARE the Python side. We don't need to build the bridge — our BrokerDataAdapter directly fetches from Deriv/FTMO APIs. Parts 9-10 show what complexity traders accept to reach Python. We start there.

data_requirements.name: d1_ohlcv_10_days
data_requirements.source: MQL5 iHigh/iLow/iOpen/iClose/iVolume → CSV string → HTTP POST

mql5_side: Loop 10 daily bars: collect OHLCV per bar
Format as CSV string with header row
Parse JSON response: signal, average_price, average_volume, explanation
If signal changed from last: update chart display
python_side: Validate columns present (date, prev_high, prev_low, prev_open, prev_close, prev_volume)
average_price = mean((high + low + open + close) / 4) across all 10 rows

steps.name: collect_daily_ohlcv
steps.description: MQL5 collects 10 D1 bars of OHLCV and formats as CSV

steps.name: http_post_to_python
steps.description: MQL5 POSTs CSV string to Flask /analyze endpoint

steps.name: python_analysis
steps.description: Python calculates 10-day average price and compares to last close

steps.name: display_on_chart
steps.description: MQL5 receives JSON response, parses fields, updates chart Comment() overlay

bridge_architecture.data_flow: MT5 → CSV string → HTTP POST → Flask → Pandas → JSON → MT5
weakness_of_this_approach: Requires running two processes simultaneously (MT5 + Python)
HTTP roundtrip latency for every signal update
CSV is fragile — no schema enforcement, breaks on special chars
Flask server must be manually started and kept alive
No reconnect logic if Python server goes down
bridge_architecture.our_advantage: We are ENTIRELY in Python. BrokerDataAdapter connects directly to Deriv/FTMO REST APIs. No bridge needed. Zero latency between data fetch and analysis.

components_to_build.name: BrokerDataAdapter
components_to_build.note: Already built. This entire Part 9 exists to reach what our BrokerDataAdapter IS. Their HTTP bridge = our adapter call.

keep: The concept of separating data collection from signal analysis — good architecture principle
CSV/JSON serialisation patterns for market data exchange
10-day lookback window for short-term directional bias
Signal throttling by time interval — prevents recalculating on every tick
Response structure: signal + average_price + average_volume + explanation
discard: The entire HTTP bridge — unnecessary when built in Python natively
Flask server dependency — we use direct function calls
CSV as data format — use structured Pandas DataFrames internally
MQL5 WebRequest, FileOpen, FileWriteString — replaced by BrokerDataAdapter
The signal logic (last_close vs 10-day average) — far too simple for production
improve: Replace avg_price signal with our full gate chain: zone + EMA + ADX + VWV + departure
Replace D1 with M15 + M1 timeframe pair for intraday precision
Add ATR-based SL/TP — this tool generates signals with no risk management
Add structure context — BULLISH/BEARISH from StructureEngine, not just avg_price comparison
Add zone identification — are we near supply/demand? That's the real signal, not avg_price
Eliminate the bridge entirely — pure Python with broker adapter

creviaderiv_insights.we_are_the_python_side: Parts 9-12 are the series struggling to reach Python capabilities. Our entire system IS Python. We don't need the bridge. Our architecture is what they're trying to build toward.
creviaderiv_insights.broker_adapter_validation: Their HTTP bridge (MQL5 → Flask) is functionally identical to our BrokerDataAdapter (Python → Deriv REST API). Same pattern, we just start from the Python side.
creviaderiv_insights.signal_logic_baseline: last_close > 10-day average is the simplest possible directional signal. It's the FLOOR of what a signal engine should do. Our zone+EMA+ADX+VWV+departure chain is many orders of magnitude more sophisticated.

article_figures.title: MQL5-Python Integration Flow
article_figures.insight: Shows the full data flow: MT5 EA → HTTP POST → Python Flask → JSON response → MT5 display. This IS our BrokerDataAdapter pattern, just implemented backwards (from MT5 side instead of Python side).

article_figures.title: WebRequest Flow Diagram
article_figures.insight: HTTP POST detail. Request body = CSV string. Response = JSON. Shows why CSV is fragile — author had to manually handle special chars and encoding.

article_figures.title: Chart Display Result
article_figures.insight: MT5 chart with signal overlay. Shows BUY signal, average price, average volume displayed via Comment().

article_figures.title: Trade Example on M1
article_figures.insight: Winning trade aligned with the D1 signal. Confirms the D1 directional bias → M1 execution pattern — same as our M15 structure → M1 entry approach.
